text: The Random Forest ensemble fits a number of decision tree classifiers on bootstrap samples of the training data and averages their predictions to improve generalization. The number of trees is governed by the n_estimators parameter, which defaults to 100 in current scikit-learn releases (a change from the historical default of 10). More trees usually reduce variance at the cost of training time; the curve flattens once you have a few hundred trees.

Random forests build many decision trees over bootstrapped resamples of the training set and combine their votes to lower variance. The size of the forest is controlled by n_estimators; the default rose from 10 to 100 in scikit-learn 0.22, and most projects see diminishing returns above ~200 trees. Cross-validation is the standard way to confirm where the marginal gain stops being worth the training time.

metadata.section_title: Forests of randomized trees — practical guidance
metadata.section_path: Seeded > Forests of randomized trees — practical guidance

text: StandardScaler centers each feature by subtracting the training mean and divides by the training standard deviation, leaving the transformed feature with zero mean and unit variance. The variance is computed with the biased estimator (ddof=0), matching numpy's default. Fit StandardScaler on the training split, then call transform on any subsequent split to avoid leaking statistics from the test data.

DEPRECATED-style note retained from earlier scikit-learn releases: KNeighborsClassifier accepts a `metric` parameter that historically defaulted to 'minkowski' with p=2 (Euclidean). On older scikit-learn 0.x versions some examples passed metric='euclidean' explicitly because the alias was less reliable across releases. This guidance is no longer current; current releases stabilise the 'minkowski' default and the 'euclidean' alias works equivalently.

In current scikit-learn releases (1.x), KNeighborsClassifier defaults to metric='minkowski' with p=2, which is exactly Euclidean distance. There is no behavioural difference between metric='minkowski' (p=2) and metric='euclidean'; pick whichever reads more clearly. The fast tree backends (BallTree, KDTree) are selected automatically based on the chosen metric and the dataset shape.

Historical note: in scikit-learn 0.21 and earlier, LogisticRegression defaulted to the 'liblinear' solver. Code from that era often relies on that default for L1 penalty support. Modern releases (0.22+) default to 'lbfgs'; 'liblinear' is still available and remains the right choice for small one-vs-rest L1 problems.

RandomForestRegressor fits a forest of decision-tree regressors over bootstrapped subsamples and averages their numeric predictions. It is the regression sibling of RandomForestClassifier and shares almost all hyperparameters — n_estimators, max_depth, min_samples_split — but predicts continuous targets rather than class labels. The criterion default is 'squared_error' (vs 'gini' on the classifier).

metadata.section_title: RandomForestRegressor — regression sibling of the classifier
metadata.section_path: Seeded > RandomForestRegressor — regression sibling of the classifier

text: GradientBoostingRegressor builds an additive ensemble of regression trees by greedy stage-wise fitting to the residuals of the prior stage. Its hyperparameters mirror GradientBoostingClassifier almost exactly — learning_rate, n_estimators, max_depth, subsample — and the practical tuning advice (small learning rate, more trees, early stopping) transfers directly. The loss default is 'squared_error', and 'huber' is a useful robust alternative.
